title: Combination Sum IV
problem_statement: Given an array of distinct integers nums and a target integer target, return the number of possible combinations that add up to target. The order of the numbers in the combination matters. You may use the same number from nums as many times as needed.

examples.explanation: No combination of 9 can sum to 3.

constraints: All values in nums are distinct.
intuition: Think of the target as a position on a number line. For each sum, count how many ordered sequences of nums reach that sum from smaller sums.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try every number at each step and subtract it from the remaining target until the target reaches zero or negative.
approaches.why_not: Exponential time due to exploring all ordered combinations. It will TLE for larger target values.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Cache the number of combinations for each remaining target to avoid recomputing duplicate subproblems.
approaches.why_not: Still recursive, but avoids repeated work. Bottom-up is simpler and avoids recursion overhead.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Iteratively build dp[sum] for all sums from 0 to target using previously computed values.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution for ordered combination counting.

interview_qa.q: Why does order matter in this problem?
interview_qa.a: Because sequences like [1,2,1] and [1,1,2] are counted as different combinations, even though they use the same numbers.
It represents the number of ordered combinations that sum exactly to t.
It avoids recursion and efficiently reuses previously computed counts for smaller targets.
dp[sum] = sum(dp[sum - num]) for each num in nums where sum >= num.
